summary:
Cossack Uprisings (c. 1600–1775 AD) were a series of major armed revolts by the Cossack peoples of the Ukrainian steppe and Don River region — autonomous communities of frontiersmen who had developed a distinctive military and democratic culture on the margins of the Polish-Lithuanian, Russian, and Ottoman empires — against the encroachment of state authority, serfdom, and the Polish Catholic domination of Orthodox Ukrainian society. The Cossacks (from the Turkic 'qazaq,' meaning free person) organized themselves in military brotherhoods (the Zaporozhian Sich on the Dnieper River being the most famous) with elected leaders (hetmans or atamans) and a tradition of autonomy that made them both valuable military assets to neighboring powers and persistent sources of revolt.

The major uprisings included: the [[event:3630|Khmelnytsky Uprising]] (1648–1657), which created the autonomous Hetmanate of the Ukrainian Cossacks and allied with Russia against Poland; the Stenka Razin Revolt (1670–1671), a massive Don Cossack peasant uprising against Russian boyar authority; and the [[event:3281|Pugachev Rebellion]] (1773–1775), the last great Cossack-peasant revolt before Catherine the Great systematically destroyed the Zaporozhian Sich (1775) and integrated the Cossack territories into the Russian imperial system. The Cossack uprisings left profound marks on Ukrainian, Polish, and Russian national identities — Khmelnytsky remains a founding hero of Ukrainian national consciousness, while the tradition of Cossack democratic autonomy influenced later Ukrainian political culture.
